content.description: Some verb, noun and adjective phrases are always followed by the *-ing* form.

content.subtitle: Example
items: ✓ She's finished **packing** her bags for the trip.

items.note: Some of the verbs, nouns and adjectives in the list above can also be followed by an object before the *-ing* form.

items.correct: I can't stand **tourists** littering in the national park.

items.note: When we put a verb after a preposition, we almost always use an *-ing* form.

items.correct: I'm excited **about visiting** the new nature reserve.

content.description: Some verb, noun and adjective phrases are usually followed by the full infinitive.

content.subtitle: Example
items: ✓ The trail appears **to be** very well marked.

items.note: Some of the verbs, nouns and adjectives in the list above can also be followed by an object before the full infinitive.

items.correct: I didn't want to miss the concert.
My coach didn't want **me** to miss the training session.

content.description: Some verbs can be followed by an object + the bare infinitive.

content.subtitle: Example
items: ✓ The ranger never lets us **approach** the wildlife.

items.note: The verbs *feel*, *hear*, *notice*, *see* and *watch* can also be followed by the *-ing* form. We often use the bare infinitive for a completed action (from start to finish). We often use the *-ing* form for an action in progress at the time.

items.correct: I heard the guide **warn** the hikers not to go off the path. (= I heard all of it.)
I heard the guide **warning** the hikers not to go off the path. (= I heard part of it.)

items.note: In the passive, *hear*, *make* and *see* are followed by the full infinitive.

items.correct: Active: The coach **made** us **run** an extra lap.
Passive: We **were made to run** an extra lap.

sections.title: Verb + full infinitive or -ing form with little or no change in meaning

content.description: Some verbs can be followed by the full infinitive or the *-ing* form with little or no change in meaning.

content.subtitle: Example
items: ✓ They continued **to play** music until late in the evening.
✓ They continued **playing** music until late in the evening.

sections.title: Verb + full infinitive or -ing form with a change in meaning

content.description: Some verbs can be followed by both the full infinitive and the *-ing* form. The choice depends on the meaning.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: ✓ I **remember** the band **playing** that song at the festival last summer!
✓ Did you **remember to buy** tickets for the concert?

rows:
I *prefer* hiking *to* cycling.
I *prefer* **travelling** by train *to* **driving** long distances.
*would prefer* + full infinitive + *rather than* (+ bare/full infinitive)
I'd *prefer* **to go** hiking on Saturday *rather than* ([to] go) on Sunday, if that suits you.
I'd *rather* **go** hiking on Saturday *than* (go) on Sunday, if that suits you.
expressing general or specific preference (about someone else)
I'd *rather* you **didn't play** music so late at night.
You'd *better* **book** your accommodation early before prices go up.

items.note: We don't usually say *I don't prefer…* We use *I prefer not to…*

items.correct: I *prefer not to* travel during the peak holiday season.

content.description: When we want to talk about someone's purpose (the reason they do something), we can use:

rows: She booked the early flight **to avoid** the rush-hour traffic.
She booked the early flight **in order to avoid** the rush-hour traffic.
She booked the early flight **so as to avoid** the rush-hour traffic.
She booked the early flight **so (that) she could avoid** the rush-hour traffic.

items.note: With a negative purpose we don't normally use the full infinitive on its own.

items.correct: She left early **in order not to** miss the opening ceremony.
She left early **so as not to** miss the opening ceremony.

items.incorrect: She left early **not to** miss the opening ceremony.

sections.title: A: -ing or infinitive
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: He doesn't feel like **going / to go** for a run in this weather. Do you?
Everyone expected the new band **failing / to fail** before releasing their first album.
What would you like **doing / to do** after the hike tomorrow?
We discussed **turning / to turn** the old barn into a rehearsal studio with the architect.
The injured player wasn't able **speaking / to speak** very clearly after the collision.
Do you mind **moving / to move** your tent, please? You're blocking the path to the beach.
The band decided to postpone **making / to make** a decision about the tour until next month.
Are you planning of **getting / to get** new ski equipment this winter?
Are you thinking **of getting / to get** a season pass for the ski resort?
I'm very pleased **telling / to tell** you that you've been selected for the national team!
The travel agency has offered **putting / to put** us up in a hotel for the night.
I'm really looking forward **to going / to go** on the safari next month.

sections.title: B: Complete using correct form
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form (-ing form or full infinitive) of the verb in brackets. You may need to use the passive voice.

items.sentence: I'll never forgive Tom for (lie) ...... about his injury to avoid training.
Paul's not very good at (make) ...... conversation with people he's just met at the hostel.
I can't resist (buy) ...... souvenirs whenever I visit a new country.
We need to encourage young athletes (study) ...... their technique, not just train harder.
I don't know how they managed (persuade) ...... the council to fund the new sports centre.
Does Maria dislike (walk) ...... so much that she refuses to explore the old town on foot?
Do you deny (steal) ...... that fishing spot? Several people saw you there.
The fans were pretending (be) ...... calm, but they were clearly nervous before the final.
The champion refused (accept) ...... that his world record had been broken.
No one understands how Luke can afford (go) ...... to so many music festivals each year.
The striker only just escaped (send) ...... off. Next time, the referee won't be so lenient.
She expected (give) ...... the lead role in the show, but was offered a minor part instead.

sections.title: C: Complete the report
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verbs in the box.
sections.passage:
Marco Torres sometimes seems to be afraid of (1) ...... hard during training. He can't help (2) ...... to avoid (3) ...... anything that involves (4) ...... his full effort, particularly during early morning sessions. He seems to detest (5) ...... the game seriously, and pretends he isn't capable of (6) ...... anything of worth. This is unfortunate as, with a little more effort, Marco could succeed in (7) ...... great progress.
However, at present he frequently just wants (8) ...... the fool during team meetings. He enjoys (9) ...... jokes to his teammates. Presumably, he imagines this (10) ...... the best way to build team spirit, but in fact he often just ends up preventing the other players from (11) ...... . He has promised on several occasions (12) ...... his conduct during training, but then he just keeps on (13) ...... in exactly the same way. Recently, I have had to beg him (14) ...... and focus during tactical briefings, but it's difficult to know how to make him (15) ...... to his senses. He just doesn't seem interested in (16) ...... to my advice. I'm tempted to give up even (17) ...... that he might improve. Having said that though, Marco is an extremely talented goalkeeper!

Sarah Mills – Head Coach

sections.title: D: Same or different meaning
sections.instructions: Are the two sentences the same (YES) or different (NO) in meaning?

items.context: a I can't bear being stuck in traffic for hours!
items.sentence: b I can't bear to be stuck in traffic for hours!

items.context: a The captain went on saying how proud he was of the whole team.
items.sentence: b The captain went on to say how proud he was of the whole team.

items.context: a Have you stopped taking breaks during rehearsal yet?
items.sentence: b Have you stopped to take a break during rehearsal yet?

items.context: a Have you started warming up yet?
items.sentence: b Have you started to warm up yet?

items.context: a They continued hiking through the forest.
items.sentence: b They continued to hike through the forest.

items.context: a We all saw the photographer capturing the moment.
items.sentence: b We all saw the photographer capture the moment.

items.context: a I love hiking in the mountains on weekends.
items.sentence: b I love to hike in the mountains on weekends.

items.context: a Why don't you try adjusting your grip on the racket?
items.sentence: b Why don't you try to adjust your grip on the racket?

items.context: a I hate being delayed at the airport.
items.sentence: b I hate to be delayed at the airport.

sections.title: E: Verbs with change of meaning
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
I should have taken my camera on the hike but I didn't remember.
**forgot** I ...... my camera on the hike.

I'll always remember the first time I climbed Kilimanjaro.
**never** I'll ...... Kilimanjaro.

I must hang up my wetsuit to dry later.
**remember** I ...... up my wetsuit later.

Jake thinks that wearing a cap on stage looks really cool.
**likes** Jake ...... a cap on stage.

Elena wishes she hadn't said that to her bandmate.
**regrets** Elena ...... that to her bandmate.

I'm sorry but your festival pass has been cancelled due to the event.
**regret** I ...... your festival pass has been cancelled due to the event.

Crashing into my opponent wasn't my intention during the race!
**mean** I ...... my opponent during the race, you know!

If we sign with that record label, we'll have to do a lot more touring.
**mean** Signing with that record label ...... a lot more touring.

The team manager insisted that Jim tidy the locker room before he was allowed to leave.
**made** Jim ...... the locker room before he was allowed to leave.

I don't suppose you watched that live concert on TV last night, did you?
**happen** You ...... that live concert on TV last night, did you?

People often think that learning to ski in summer is a waste of time.
**considered** Learning to ski in summer ...... a waste of time.

sections.title: F: Error correction (Prefer, would rather, had better)
sections.instructions: Each of the words or phrases in bold is incorrect. Rewrite them correctly.

items.sentence: I generally prefer hiking **from** cycling, to be honest.
Do you prefer listening to music at home to **go** to a live concert?
She **had prefer** to rehearse in the morning, if that's all right with everyone.
He'd prefer **not have** to carry such a heavy backpack, but he will if he needs to.
I'd rather you **write** your travel blog before we set off on the next leg of the trip.
Would you prefer to stay at the hostel rather **from** book a hotel tonight?
You **would better** pack sunscreen if you're hiking in the desert.
I **had rather** watch the sunset from the beach than from the hotel balcony.
I'd prefer **having** an early start tomorrow morning, if that suits everyone.
She'd **rather start** training seriously if she wants to finish the marathon next month.
Tom prefers **to not** use poles when he goes skiing.

sections.title: G: Write one word in each gap
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: I prefer jazz music ...... classical, to be honest.
I would prefer to visit the national park on Friday ...... than on the weekend.
I ...... cycling through the countryside to driving on the motorway.
I'd rather ...... to a jazz bar than a noisy nightclub.
I'd ...... you didn't practise your drums while I'm trying to concentrate.
You'd ...... book your flights soon as seats are filling up fast.
We set off at dawn in ...... to reach the summit before noon.
We stayed on the marked trail so ...... not to disturb the nesting wildlife.
I called the venue ...... find out what time the doors opened.
I actually prefer ...... to listen to music through earphones; it sounds far better through proper speakers.
Don't you think we ...... better turn the music down a bit before the neighbours complain?

sections.title: H: Multiple choice
sections.passage:
Athletes appear (1) ...... in very different ways. Some athletes expect (2) ...... errors during competitions and are capable of (3) ...... from their setbacks. They don't mind (4) ...... by their coach and often ask (5) ...... given feedback after every session.
Others, however, dislike (6) ...... errors in front of their teammates. They try to avoid (7) ...... anything at which they might perform poorly. They would rather (8) ...... a skill in isolation and be sure they have mastered it (9) ...... attempt a challenge based on an area they don't feel they have finished (10) ...... yet.
Both approaches seem (11) ...... equally effective, but combining the two often produces the best results. In (12) ...... to develop effectively, athletes have to remember (13) ...... risks sometimes. But they also need to feel confident and secure in what they're doing so (14) ...... not to lose their motivation. All athletes should at least think about (15) ...... the way that they approach their training.
